Fix colorless card discovery for `id=c` and related color identity searches (#441)

Colorless cards are stored in the DB with `card_color_identity = {}`
(empty JSONB), mirroring Scryfall's `color_identity: []`. The
`get_colors_comparison_object` function was mapping `"c"` → `{"C":
True}`, so `id=c` generated `card_color_identity = {"C": True}` —
matching nothing.

## Changes

- **`api/parsing/card_query_nodes.py`** — Strip `"c"` from the color
code dict in `get_colors_comparison_object`, returning `{}` for
colorless instead of `{"C": True}`. Works for both the letter-code path
(`"c"`) and the name path (`"colorless"`).

```python
# Before
return {c.upper(): True for c in val}           # "c" → {"C": True}

# After
return {c.upper(): True for c in val if c != "c"}  # "c" → {}
```

- **`api/parsing/tests/test_sql_gen.py`** — Add parametrized cases for
`id=c`, `id:c`, `id:colorless`, and `id=colorless`, all asserting the
comparison value is `{}`.

All operators now behave correctly:

| Query | SQL fragment | Semantics |
|-------|-------------|-----------|
| `id=c` | `card_color_identity = {}` | exactly colorless |
| `id:c` / `id<=c` | `card_color_identity <@ {}` | only colorless
(subsets of empty) |
| `id>=c` | `card_color_identity @> {}` | all cards (every set contains
∅) |
| `id>c` | `@> {} AND <> {}` | any colored identity |

feat: lock AssemblyVersion to major, FileVersion to full numeric version

Adds src/Directory.Build.targets (imported after SDK targets, so $(Version)
is fully resolved) with two overrides for all library projects:

  AssemblyVersion = major.0.0.0
    Locked to the major version so consumers compiled against v1.0 don't
    need to recompile for v1.1/v1.2 (binding-redirect / strong-name safety).

  FileVersion = major.minor.patch.0
    Pre-release label stripped (regex match on '^\d+\.\d+\.\d+') because
    Windows file-version fields only accept numeric components.

  InformationalVersion = x.y.z[-pre]+<commit-sha>
    Left to the SDK default — carries the full SemVer label plus source hash.

Verified against three cases:
  -p:Version=1.2.3      → AV=1.0.0.0  FV=1.2.3.0   IV=1.2.3+sha
  -p:Version=2.0.0-beta.1 → AV=2.0.0.0  FV=2.0.0.0   IV=2.0.0-beta.1+sha
  default (0.1.0)       → AV=0.0.0.0  FV=0.1.0.0   IV=0.1.0+sha

fix: force TERM=dumb for gh auth PTY, extend timeout to 60s

gh auth login uses bubbletea TUI which emits cursor-positioning
ANSI sequences. With TERM set to a capable value, the fancy UI
draws over itself with escape codes that our sanitizer can't
reliably reconstruct into recognisable strings like
'Login with a web browser'. Setting TERM=dumb + NO_COLOR=1 forces
gh into plain-text fallback mode so the prompt text appears as
literal printable characters.

Also:
- Extend code wait deadline from 30s to 60s
- Log PTY OSErrors at WARNING instead of silently swallowing them
- Dump the sanitised PTY buffer in the timeout warning so failures
  are visible without needing DEBUG logging enabled
- Broaden Stage-1 match: 'web browser' (case-insensitive) in
  addition to exact phrase matches
- Add per-iteration DEBUG log of PTY content for future diagnosis

feat: implement real Copilot API call in /api/ask (0.1.1)

Replace the hardcoded stub response with an actual call to the
GitHub Copilot chat completions API (api.githubcopilot.com) —
the same API that powers the GitHub Copilot CLI.

How it works:
1. The stored GitHub OAuth token (from the auth flow) is exchanged
   for a short-lived Copilot API token via
   api.github.com/copilot_internal/v2/token (cached, auto-refreshed).
2. The prompt + system prompt are sent to
   api.githubcopilot.com/chat/completions (OpenAI-compatible).
3. If a Home Assistant MCP server URL is configured, it is included
   in the system prompt so the model knows about it.
4. The real response text is returned to HA Assistant/Assist.

Also:
- Increase api.py ask timeout from 10s to 120s (LLM calls take time)
- Bump to 0.1.1

gh auth login --web forces a PKCE web flow with a localhost redirect
that can never complete inside a headless container. Switch to the
standard interactive device code flow used by GitHub Copilot CLI:

  gh auth login --hostname <host> --git-protocol https ...

The PTY driver now uses a two-stage state machine:
  Stage 1 - detect the 'How would you like to authenticate?' prompt
            and press Enter to select 'Login with a web browser'
  Stage 2 - wait for the device code (XXXX-XXXX), capture it, then
            press Enter so gh opens https://github.com/login/device

The user navigates to github.com/login/device, enters the code, and
gh polls for completion — no localhost redirect needed.

Bump to 0.1.0.6.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

fix: clear stale pending auth state with null user_code

Three places were preventing the auth code from ever appearing:

1. Bridge startup: if the persisted auth state file contained a
   pending_device_flow with user_code=null (left from a previous
   crashed/timed-out attempt), it was restored and surfaced to HA
   as 'already pending'. Now cleared on startup.

2. Bridge /auth/device/start: if the gh process was running but
   produced no code, the endpoint returned the null-code state
   unchanged instead of restarting. Now kills and restarts.

3. config_flow: a pending state with user_code=None was treated as
   a valid 'already pending' flow, so /auth/device/start was never
   called. Now only reuses a pending state that has a real code.

Bump to 0.1.0.5.

fix: wait for one-time code before pressing Enter in gh auth login

The gh auth login --web flow prints the one-time device code first,
then waits for Enter before opening the browser. Previously, Enter was
sent immediately after spawning the process, before any output was
read. This caused the code to never be captured, so the HA UI showed
no code or verification URL.

Fix: poll gh output for up to 20 seconds waiting for the code to
appear. Once captured, send Enter so gh proceeds with the auth flow.
If no code appears within the timeout, send Enter anyway and let
subsequent polls pick it up.

Also bump versions to 0.1.0.4.

Add portability and generated-asset quality gates for VS Code installs (#19)

This change adds repo-level controls to catch environment-specific data
and malformed generated assets before they ship. It also verifies that a
copied WhytCard setup remains installable and structurally valid across
target environments, including the VS Code-specific files the repo
generates.

- **Portability guardrails**
- adds a dedicated `vscode-install-portability` workflow that installs
the generated bundle into a fresh target repo on Ubuntu and Windows
- validates the installed `.github/` and `.vscode/` layout instead of
only checking source files in this repo

- **Generated VS Code asset validation**
- adds `scripts/validate-vscode-output.js` to validate generated agents,
prompts, instructions, hook scripts, and `mcp.json`
- checks frontmatter shape, required hook wiring, expected script set,
unresolved template tokens, and non-portable text patterns
- verifies that the generated prompt list matches the embedded command
allowlist used by the VS Code prompt-dispatch hook

- **CI workflow tightening**
- extends `plugin-quality.yml` to build the VS Code bundle before
validation
- runs the new VS Code output validator as part of the main quality gate
- adds Cursor hook schema validation alongside the existing standalone
and smoke checks

- **Installer completeness**
- updates the VS Code install scripts to copy `vscode/mcp.json` into the
target repo’s `.vscode/mcp.json`
- closes the gap between documented install output and actual installed
files

- **Validator compatibility fixes**
- updates Cursor hook validation to allow environment-variable-based
command targets used by installed hooks
- keeps standalone audit coverage while avoiding false positives on the
new validation script

Example of the new validation entrypoint:

```bash
node ./scripts/validate-vscode-output.js
node ./scripts/validate-vscode-output.js --project-root "/path/to/copied-repo"
```

commit.message:
fix: route non-success streaming responses through buffered error path (#1348)

## Summary

When a streaming request (`stream: true` in OpenAI Chat Completions, or
`stream: true` in Anthropic Messages) receives a non-2xx error response
from the backend, the error response body is silently dropped. The
client receives the correct status code and headers (including
`Content-Length`) but zero body bytes, causing `unexpected EOF` /
`connection closed before message completed`.

## Root Cause

`process_response` dispatches to `process_streaming` based solely on
`req.streaming`, without checking the response status:

```rust
if req.streaming {
    return self.process_streaming(req, ..., resp).await;
}
```

Backend error responses (4xx, 5xx) are plain JSON (`application/json`),
not event-stream data. The streaming parsers — specifically the AWS
EventStream decoder for Bedrock — interpret the JSON bytes as binary
frame headers. For example, `{"me` (the start of `{"message":"..."}`')
is read as a big-endian u32 frame length of ~2 GB. The decoder returns
`Incomplete` forever, producing zero output bytes.

Meanwhile, the original `Content-Length` header from the backend is
preserved through `resp.map(|b| ...)`. The client sees `Content-Length:
116` but receives 0 bytes → `unexpected EOF`.

The non-streaming path correctly handles errors via `process_error` →
`translate_error`, but is unreachable when `req.streaming == true`.

## Fix

Add a response status check before entering the streaming path:

```rust
if req.streaming && resp.status().is_success() {
    return self.process_streaming(req, ..., resp).await;
}
```

Non-success responses now fall through to the buffered path where
`process_error` translates the backend error format to the frontend
format and the body is forwarded intact.

## Impact

This affects **every** streaming request to any backend that returns
plain JSON error responses — which is standard behavior for AWS Bedrock,
and likely other providers. Without this fix, clients cannot read error
details from streaming requests, making debugging backend rejections
(e.g. `ValidationException`) impossible.

Fixes #1340

---------

Co-authored-by: Copilot <175728472+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>
Co-authored-by: Devin <158243242+devin-ai-integration[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Update .github/workflows/ci.yml

Co-authored-by: Copilot <175728472+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Unix Launch Fixes (#4304)

* Gitignore for unix objects and bins

* libmpg123 mac

* try with kosmiskrisp instead of molten xx, much less problematic?

* Explicit cast for box3d and include for utlblockmemory to make clang happ

* Simple DLLImportResolver for the 3 platforms, on linux/mac it tries to load DLLs from managed/ directory even with PATH being set, and backport the steamworks behaviour from Facepunch.Steamwokrs

* Dont ShaderCompile outside of tools, resourcecompiler is not even enabled there

* Only enable steam audio binaural effect on windows, we self compile it with that feature on there, dont bother yet for linux/mac until we have a better way to do dependency track

* Fix DLLImportResolver, also let's try to keep just a single set of binaries for managed without platform variants

* Fix rect_t interop on ARM64

On ARM64, NativeRect (4 floats = HFA) gets passed in float registers (v0-v3), but the C++ function reads from x1 expecting a pointer → null crash.
On x64 Windows, passing a 16-byte struct "by value" through an unmanaged function pointer accidentally works because the x64 ABI passes it by hidden pointer anyway. On ARM64, NativeRect (4 floats = HFA) goes into float registers v0-v3, leaving x1=0x0 for the C++ Rect_t* parameter.

* Building the load path via string interpolation hardcodes "/" and can produce double-separators because NetCore.NativeDllPath already includes a trailing slash. For consistency with the rest of the codebase (e.g., InteropGen uses Path.Combine with NativeDllPath) and to avoid path quirks across platforms, use Path.Combine(NetCore.NativeDllPath, nativeName).
